text.title: STATE, AMPP, DESC, and WWWF: sharing and closing
text.summary: STATE structures the opening (Share facts → Tell story → Ask their path → Talk tentatively → Encourage testing); AMPP structures listening; DESC is for written boundary-setting; WWWF closes with specific commitments.

rule.statement: Open with observable facts (not interpretations), share your tentative story, then immediately invite the other person's perspective. The opening must be deliverable in under 60 seconds — 3-5 sentences maximum.
rule.rationale: Openings longer than 60 seconds read as monologues. "Talk tentatively" ("I might be missing something") is not weakness — it is the signal that invites the other party to contribute.

example.code: S: "The last three milestones were delivered 2-3 days late." (Share facts — observable) T: "I'm starting to worry about whether we'll hit the launch date." (Tell story — tentative) A: "Can you help me understand what's happening?" (Ask for their path) T: "I might be missing context." (Talk tentatively) E: "I'm open to hearing I'm wrong about this." (Encourage testing)

antipattern.description: Ending the STATE opening with a rhetorical question ("You see that this is a problem, right?") instead of a genuine inquiry.
antipattern.reason: Rhetorical questions destroy the safety that the rest of STATE was building. The other person reads them as a trap.

rule.statement: Use AMPP to understand the other person's perspective: Ask (open questions), Mirror (reflect emotions back), Paraphrase (restate content), Prime (guess their feeling if they seem stuck).
rule.rationale: AMPP prevents the common failure of preparing your rebuttal while the other person is still speaking. Genuine listening requires structured attention.

list.item: Ask: "What's your view?" — open the door
Mirror: "You seem frustrated about this." — reflect what you observe
Paraphrase: "So you're saying the timeline was unrealistic from the start?" — confirm
Prime: "Are you worried about how this will affect your team's workload?" — guess if stuck

section.title: DESC — written boundary-setting

rule.statement: Use DESC for written communication (Slack, email) when a live conversation is not possible or to follow up after one. Never read DESC aloud in a meeting as a script.
rule.rationale: DESC is calibrated for written async delivery. Read aloud, it sounds prepared and clinical — destroying the rapport required for a live difficult conversation.

example.code: D (Describe): "When I receive Slack messages at 10pm expecting an immediate response..." E (Express): "I feel stressed and unable to disconnect from work..." S (Specify): "I would like us to agree that non-urgent messages can wait until morning..." C (Consequences): "This would help me be more focused during work hours. If it continues, I'll need to turn off notifications entirely."

section.reference: Prompt for generating a DESC script

section.title: Skill 7: WWWF — move to action

rule.statement: End every difficult conversation with WHO does WHAT by WHEN and a scheduled follow-up date. Vague commitment ("we'll do better") is not an agreement.
rule.rationale: Without specific accountability, both parties leave with different interpretations of what was agreed. Follow-up date closes the loop and signals the issue is being tracked.

section.reference: Prompt for generating a STATE opening script
